column1: This is the "Sociological Spine" that the University of Szczecin adds to the technical curriculum.
Before entering the matrix, these are the core values derived from the texts that guide the sociological interventions:

column2: Intergenerational Equity: Protecting the ocean for future "People of the Sea."
Procedural Justice: Fair inclusion in decision-making (not just consultation).
Solidarity: The port/coast as a shared community asset, not just a corporate zone.
Stewardship: Moving from "extracting resources" to "caretaking ecosystems."
Trans-corporeality: Acknowledging that human health and ocean health are biologically linked (Marinisation).

column3:
Theory: Marinisation (Physiological)

Understanding how marine bio-compounds affect human health (e.g., in food/pharma).
column4:
Value: Trans-corporeality

Justification: Students must understand we are not separate from the ocean; what we put in (pollution) returns to us (in food).

column3:
Concept: Benefit Sharing / Blue Justice

Ensuring local communities profit from genetic data extraction.
column4:
Value: Equity

Justification: Prevents "Biopiracy." Technical access to genes is useless if legal/ethical theft leads to international bans.

column3:
Theory: Occupational Identification

Transitioning the "Fisher" identity to "Ocean Farmer/Guardian."
column4:
Value: Dignity

Justification: Fishers often resist regulation because it threatens their identity, not just income. We teach how to evolve that identity.

column1: Cluster 2: Energy, Resources & Desalination (The Hard Economy)

column3:
Theory: Maritime Territorial Community

Treating the sea as a "lived space" (Territory) rather than empty "space" to be zoned.
column4:
Value: Recognition

Justification: Engineers see coordinates; locals see heritage. Ignoring the "Territorial" aspect of the sea causes NIMBYism and protests.

column3:
Theory: The Marine Factor (Agency)

Recognizing the ocean’s capacity to destroy infrastructure if limits are crossed.
column4:
Value: Precautionary Principle

Justification: Deep-sea mining risks are unknown. Sociology teaches respect for the "Agency of Nature" (Marinisation) as a hard limit.

column3:
Concept: Social License to Operate (SLO)

Moving from "Permitting" (Legal) to "Acceptance" (Social).
column4:
Value: Procedural Justice

Justification: A legal permit does not stop sabotage or litigation. SLO is the "soft" permit granted by the community.

column1: Cluster 3: Transport, Ports & Infrastructure (The Blue Spine)
Focus: Re-connecting the City and the Sea

column3:
Theory: De-maritimisation

Diagnosing the cultural/physical separation of the city from its port due to containerization.
column4:
Value: Cultural Heritage

Justification: A port that is culturally invisible to its city loses political support. We teach how to reverse this alienation.

column3:
Concept: Re-maritimisation

Active policies to reintegrate port culture into civic life (e.g., open waterfronts, festivals).
column4:
Value: Solidarity

Justification: Rebuilding the "Maritime Community" ensures the port is seen as a provider of identity, not just traffic and pollution.

column3:
Concept: Industrial Ecology as Social Contract

Ports as hubs for circular living, not just transit.
column4:
Value: Responsibility

Justification: The port takes responsibility for the lifecycle of ships, protecting the "Global South" from toxic scrapping (Blue Justice).

column3:
Concept: Blue Citizenship

Transforming tourists from "consumers of views" to "citizens of the ocean."
column4:
Value: Stewardship

Justification: Tourism is extractive unless it builds a "Stewardship" mindset. We teach how to use tech (VR) to build empathy with the ocean.

column3:
Theory: Trans-Oceanisation

Planetary governance where the ocean is a single, shared security space.
column4:
Value: Peace & Cooperation

Justification: Security is no longer just national; it is planetary (climate security). This requires a "One Ocean" diplomatic mindset.

column3:
Concept: The Oceanic Turn (Sociology)

Understanding data not as numbers, but as the "Voice of the Ocean."
column4:
Value: Transparency

Justification: Who owns ocean data? Sociology ensures data is a public good (Open Science) rather than privatized knowledge.
